reasoning: The CCC high-yield spread recently spiked from the mid-7s in February to a peak of 10.58 in late April, and has been sharply reverting since then (down to 9.33 by May 9). Since the data demonstrates a clear downward reversion trend over the last 3 weeks following what appears to be a sudden, likely idiosyncratic shock in April, the spread is more likely than not to continue falling linearly toward its historical baseline around ~8.0. Therefore, the probability of the spread increasing compared to its May 11 value is less than 0.50, especially over the short term (May 18). Over longer horizons, uncertainty increases and the spread could experience new macro shocks or business cycle downturns, so probabilities gradually approach 0.50 for the more distant resolution dates (June, August, and November).
